verses.commentary: Paul reminds them of the gospel he preached, which they received and on which they stand. The gospel is foundation, not mere introduction. Standing requires continued adherence.
Salvation depends on holding firmly to the gospel. Otherwise, belief was in vain. Perseverance demonstrates genuine faith; apostasy reveals false profession.
Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures. The gospel's first plank: substitutionary atonement grounded in Old Testament prophecy. Death was purposeful, not accidental.
Burial confirms real death; resurrection on the third day fulfills Scripture. Both events are historical and theological. The gospel rests on verifiable facts.
Christ appeared to Peter, then to the Twelve. Eyewitness testimony establishes resurrection. Personal encounters with the risen Christ form the foundation of apostolic proclamation.
More than 500 witnesses at once, most still living. Paul invites verification. The resurrection is public, witnessed, and falsifiable if untrue.
Jesus appeared to James, then to all the apostles. Multiple independent witnesses across different occasions. The evidence is cumulative and diverse.
Last of all, Christ appeared to Paul, as to one abnormally born. Paul's apostleship rests on resurrection encounter. His experience, though unique, is legitimate.
Paul is least of apostles, unworthy of the title because he persecuted the church. Humility acknowledges his violent past. Grace alone qualifies him.
By God's grace Paul is what he is. Grace wasn't wasted—he worked harder than all. Yet not he, but grace working through him. Synergism of divine empowerment and human effort.
Whether Paul or the other apostles, the message is the same. Unity in gospel content despite diversity of messengers. This is what they believed.
If Christ is preached as raised, how can some deny resurrection of the dead? Logical inconsistency. Christ's resurrection implies general resurrection.
If no resurrection of the dead, then Christ hasn't been raised. The logic is reciprocal. Denying one undermines the other.
If Christ isn't raised, preaching is useless and faith is futile. The entire Christian edifice collapses without resurrection. Faith in a dead Christ saves no one.
Apostles would be false witnesses about God, testifying that He raised Christ when He didn't. If the dead aren't raised, apostolic testimony is fraudulent.
For if the dead aren't raised, Christ hasn't been raised either. The principle applies universally. Christ's resurrection and general resurrection stand or fall together.
If Christ isn't raised, faith is futile and you're still in your sins. Atonement without resurrection is incomplete. Victory over sin requires victory over death.
Those who died in Christ are lost if there's no resurrection. Death would be final defeat, not temporary sleep. Hope expires at the grave.
If hope in Christ is for this life only, we're most pitiable. Christianity without resurrection is delusion. Suffering for a lie makes believers fools.
But Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep. Firstfruits guarantee the harvest. His resurrection inaugurates general resurrection.
Death came through a man (Adam); resurrection comes through a man (Christ). The solution matches the problem. Representative heads determine corporate destiny.
In Adam all die; in Christ all will be made alive. Two humanities, two outcomes. Union with Adam brings death; union with Christ brings life.
Order of resurrection: Christ as firstfruits, then those who belong to Him at His coming. Sequential fulfillment across time. Resurrection happens in stages.
Then the end comes when Christ hands the kingdom to God the Father after destroying all dominion, authority, and power. Final consummation follows complete victory.
Christ must reign until He has put all enemies under His feet. Current session involves progressive subjugation. His rule continues until total victory.
The last enemy to be destroyed is death. Death's defeat completes redemption. Final victory over the ultimate foe.
God put everything under Christ's feet, but this excludes God Himself. Christ's authority is delegated, not absolute. The Father remains supreme.
When all is subjected, the Son will subject Himself to the Father, so God may be all in all. Christological submission doesn't contradict divinity but reflects functional order within the Godhead.
Why be baptized for the dead if the dead aren't raised? Paul references a practice without endorsing it. The logic: their behavior assumes resurrection.
Why endanger ourselves hourly if there's no resurrection? Apostolic suffering makes no sense without future hope. Risk requires reward.
Paul faces death daily. His boast in the Corinthians testifies to constant danger. Daily dying proves commitment to resurrection hope.
If Paul fought beasts in Ephesus from merely human motives, what gain? If the dead aren't raised, hedonism is rational: eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.
Bad company corrupts good character. Association shapes values. Denial of resurrection reflects and reinforces moral decay.
Come back to your senses, stop sinning. Some are ignorant of God—shameful for the church. Resurrection denial stems from spiritual ignorance and moral compromise.
How are the dead raised? With what body? The skeptic's questions demand answers. Paul will explain resurrection continuity and transformation.
Foolish question. What you sow doesn't come to life unless it dies. Agricultural analogy: death precedes new life. Apparent end enables transformation.
You don't plant the body that will be but a bare seed. Identity persists through transformation. What emerges differs from what was sown.
God gives it a body as He determined, each seed its own body. Divine sovereignty governs resurrection form. Continuity and transformation coexist by God's design.
Not all flesh is the same: people, animals, birds, fish differ. Variety in created order illustrates diverse resurrection forms. Difference doesn't mean discontinuity.
Heavenly and earthly bodies have different splendors. Celestial glory differs from terrestrial glory. The resurrection body will be suited to its new environment.
Sun, moon, and stars differ in splendor; stars differ from each other. Gradations of glory illustrate resurrection diversity. Unity doesn't require uniformity.
The body sown is perishable, raised imperishable. Mortality yields to immortality. Decay and death no longer touch the resurrection body.
Sown in dishonor, raised in glory. Sown in weakness, raised in power. Transformation from shame and frailty to honor and strength.
Sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body. If there's a natural body, there's also a spiritual body. Not immaterial but Spirit-empowered, suited for eternal life.
The first Adam became a living being; the last Adam a life-giving spirit. Adam brought natural life; Christ brings resurrection life. Christ reverses Adam's legacy.
The spiritual didn't come first but the natural, then the spiritual. Order matters: earthly precedes heavenly. Creation anticipates new creation.
The first man was from dust; the second man is from heaven. Origin determines nature. Adam was earthbound; Christ is heavenly.
As the earthly man was, so are those of earth. As the heavenly man is, so are those of heaven. Identity flows from representative head.
We bore the image of the earthly; we shall bear the image of the heavenly. Past and future identity. Conformity to Adam yields to conformity to Christ.
Flesh and blood cannot inherit God's kingdom; the perishable cannot inherit the imperishable. Transformation is necessary, not optional. Current bodies are unsuited for eternal life.
A mystery: we won't all sleep, but we'll all be changed. Not all will die, but all will be transformed. Death isn't required for resurrection bodies.
In a flash, at the last trumpet, the dead will be raised imperishable, and we'll be changed. Instantaneous transformation accompanies Christ's return.
The perishable must clothe itself with the imperishable, the mortal with immortality. Necessity, not possibility. Transformation completes redemption.
When the perishable is clothed with the imperishable, then the saying will come true: Death has been swallowed up in victory. Isaiah's prophecy finds fulfillment.
Where, O death, is your victory? Where is your sting? Taunting death as defeated foe. Resurrection renders death powerless.
The sting of death is sin; the power of sin is the law. Death's terror comes through sin's guilt exposed by law. The connection: law reveals sin; sin brings death.
Thanks be to God who gives us victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. Victory is gift, not achievement. Christ alone conquers sin, law, and death.
Stand firm, unmovable, always abounding in the Lord's work. Your labor isn't in vain. Resurrection hope fuels present faithfulness. Work has eternal significance.
